title: Validating Others

pages.text:
192
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permis-
sion to photocopy this worksheet is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details). Purchasers may
download a larger version of this worksheet from www.guilford.com/dbt-worksheets.
Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 12   (I. E. Handouts 17, 18)
Validating Others
Due Date:
  Name:
 Week Starting:
Fill out this sheet whenever you practice your validation skills and whenever you have an opportunity
to practice even if you don’t (or almost don’t) do anything to practice. Write on the back of this sheet
if you need more room.
Check off types of validation that you practiced (on purpose) with others:

 1.	 Paid attention.

 2.	Reflected back what was said or done,
remaining open to correction.

 3.	Was sensitive to what was unsaid.

 4.	Expressed how what was felt, done, or said
made sense, given the causes.

 5.	Acknowledged and acted on what was valid.

 6.	Acted authentically and as an equal.
List one invalidating and two validating statements made to others.
1.
2.
3.
Describe a situation where you were nonjudgmental of someone in the past week.
Describe a situation where you used validation in the past week.
Who was the person you validated?
What exactly did you do or say to validate the person?
What was the outcome?
How did you feel afterward?
Would you say or do something differently next time? If so, what?

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Validating Others
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
